content.problemStatement:
Alice has a **binary** string SSS of length NNN. Alice and Bob decided to play a game on this string, taking turns.

On their turn, a player does the following:


If there is no palindromic cyclic substring of length >1\gt 1>1, this player loses the game.
Otherwise, the player will perform the following two-step process:

Choose any palindromic cyclic substring of SSS with length >1\gt 1>1.
Choose some character within this palindromic cyclic substring and delete it from SSS.

Note that this will reduce the length of SSS by 111.


The definition of a cyclic substring can be found below.

If Alice goes first and both players play optimally, who will win the game?

**Note**:


A palindrome is a string that reads the same forward and backward.

For example, 101, 11, 10100101\texttt{101, 11, 10100101}101, 11, 10100101 are palindromes, while 10\texttt{10}10 and 1010\texttt{1010}1010 are not.
Formally, a cyclic substring of a string SSS of length NNN is defined by two integers LLL and RRR (1≤L,R≤N1 \leq L, R \leq N1≤L,R≤N), where:

If L≤RL \leq RL≤R, the cyclic substring is SLSL+1…SRS_LS_{L+1}\ldots S_RSL​SL+1​…SR​
If L>RL \gt RL>R, the cyclic substring is SLSL+1…SNS1…SRS_LS_{L+1}\ldots S_NS_1\ldots S_RSL​SL+1​…SN​S1​…SR​
For example, if S=abcdS = \texttt{abcd}S=abcd, its cyclic substrings of length 333 are abc, bcd, cda, dab\texttt{abc, bcd, cda, dab}abc, bcd, cda, dab.



Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN — the length of SSS.
The next line contains the binary string SSS.




Output Format
For each test case, print either `"Alice"` or `"Bob"` (without quotes), denoting the winner of the game under optimal play.


Each letter of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, the strings `Bob`, `bob`, `BOB`, and `bOb` will be treated as equivalent.
constraints: SSS is a binary string, i.e, contains only the characters 000 and 111.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Alice cannot make a move, so Bob wins.
Test case 222: Alice cannot make a move, so Bob wins.
Note that the only cyclic substrings of length >1\gt 1>1 here are 10\texttt{10}10 and 01\texttt{01}01, neither of which are palindromes.
Test case 333: Alice can win via the following strategy:

First, choose the cyclic substring 010\texttt{010}010, and delete the 111 from it. The remaining string is 000\texttt{000}000.
No matter what Bob does, he can only delete a 000, resulting in 00\texttt{00}00.
Alice now chooses 00\texttt{00}00 and deletes a 000, resulting in a singleton.
Bob cannot move, so Alice wins.
